paragraphs.en: On the day Dinah lost her hat, I was sitting on the top step of my just-right Scamp trailer doing a crossword. I was puzzling over nine-across, “Thai tidbits.” Seven letters. I had A-blank-blank-E-blank-G-S. I was debating whether or not to get my phone and look it up when Morris came out of his cute little Airstream with Dinah’s pushchair in his arms.
paragraphs.zh: 在迪娜丢帽子那天，我正坐在我那间尺寸正合适的Scamp拖车楼梯最上面那级，上着字谜。我正在苦思第九横，题目是“泰国小点心”，共有七个字母。我有A-空-空-E-空-G-S。正在犹豫要不要拿出手机查一下时，莫里斯抱着迪娜的婴儿车从他那台可爱的Airstream里走了出来。

paragraphs.en: From the far end of Hallelujah Avenue, which ends in a boat landing and a lick of beach, I could hear Bob Seger’s “Heavy Music,” an earworm if ever an earworm there was. Adding insult to injury, it seemed to be on terminal repeat.
paragraphs.zh: 从霍利路（Hallelujah Avenue）尽头那段有码头和一小片海滩的地方，我听到了鲍勃·希格（Bob Seger）的《Heavy Music》，要是有“洗脑神曲”这回事，这首歌就是。更尴尬的是，它似乎还在反复终曲循环。

paragraphs.en: My name is Sherry Winfield. That’s Sherry with a y instead of an i at the end, which makes it old-school. I’m in my 70s now, and at plus-200, I’m sort of a truck. These days I’d look mighty silly in a mini, but there was a time, my friend, when I could knock your eyes out. I had moves.
paragraphs.zh: 我叫雪莉·温菲尔德（Sherry Winfield）。也就是在结尾用y不是i拼的Sherry，这听起来更老派。我现在已经七十多岁了，而且体重超过200磅左右，我算是个“吨位”了。如今我穿短款衣服会显得很笨拙，但以前啊，朋友，我可有过“让你看花眼”的时候。我那会儿可会跳。

paragraphs.en: That was then. Now I’m a widow living in the Happy Haven Trailer Park. I knit sweaters, play bridge, and attend the Mystery-Book Club every week, but—like you, reading this—I have a past. In my closet is a T-shirt that says a true thing: I MAY BE OLD BUT I SAW ALL THE GOOD BANDS. That would include Bob Seger and the Silver Bullet Band, by the way, although “Heavy Music” was never one of my favorites. I was more of a “Nutbush City Limits” gal (yes, I know Tina Turner wrote it).
paragraphs.zh: 那是从前了。现在我在“快乐港湾拖车营地”（Happy Haven Trailer Park）当寡妇住着。我会织毛衣，打桥牌，还每周参加“Mystery-Book Club”，但——和你现在读这段话一样——我也有自己的过去。在我衣柜里有一件印着一句真理的话的T恤：我可能老了，但我见过所有的好乐队。顺便说一句，这当然也包括鲍勃·塞格（Bob Seger）和银弹乐队（Silver Bullet Band），尽管《Heavy Music》从来都不是我最喜欢的。我更喜欢《Nutbush City Limits》这首歌（是的，我知道这是蒂娜·特纳（Tina Turner）写的）。

paragraphs.en: Morris carried Dinah’s chair down his three concrete-block steps and went back inside for the girl. I don’t remember what date that was, only that it was overcast and humid and close enough to Halloween that I had bought a bunch of mini candy bars at the Publix up the Tamiami for the little ghosts and goblins.
paragraphs.zh: 莫里斯抱着迪娜的椅子走下他那三级水泥块台阶，又回到里面给那个女孩拿东西。我不记得那是哪一天，只记得那天阴沉、潮湿，而且距离万圣节已经近得很，正好让我在Tamiami那头的Publix买了一大堆迷你糖果棒，拿去给小鬼怪和小妖精们。

paragraphs.en: Down the way, a bunch of young fellas were hooting and hollering. You know how they do when Friday afternoon is the launching ramp for Friday night. Back then I would have been right there with them, wearing my red bikini. Back then I had a tanned midriff you could have bounced a quarter off.
paragraphs.zh: 那边一群年轻小伙子在尖叫乱嚷。你知道的，每逢周五下午都像是周五晚上的发动坡道。那时候的我本来也会像他们一样站在那儿，穿着我的红色比基尼。那时我的小腹晒得黝黑，硬得都能把一角硬币弹回来。

paragraphs.en: I went back to my crossword. Ten-down crossed “Thai tidbits.” That one was “Connecticut river,” and let me tell you what all veteran crossword puzzlers know: The places where tough clues cross are total pissers. I could name the state capitals, I knew most of the countries in Africa, ditto “The younger Guthrie” (Arlo) and “Old English before” (ere), but “Connecticut river”? Come on. There must be a ton of rivers in the Nutmeg State.
paragraphs.zh: 我又回去做填字游戏了。十字下方有一条线索：'Thai tidbits'，答案是“Connecticut river”。我得告诉你们，所有资深填字高手都明白：难题和它的交叉点最让人抓狂。我能报出各州首府，我认识非洲大多数国家，连“更年轻的Guthrie”（Arlo）和“Old English before”（ere）也会，但“Connecticut river”？拜托。盖尔奇州（Nutmeg State）里肯定有成千上万条河。

paragraphs.en: Except I didn’t need to get off my fat ass and go look at my phone. Not when I had Morris.
paragraphs.zh: 可我根本不需要挪动我这只胖屁股去看手机。因为我有莫里斯。

paragraphs.en: He came out with his kippah perched slightly askew on his head and Dinah in his arms. If she grew a lot more, the girl would be too much for him, but the answer to both how long and how big she might grow was not very. How old was she on that overcast pre-Halloween day? I don’t know. She might have been 6; she might have been 10 or 12. Her stomach bulged beneath her I ❤ FLORIDA boatneck and her face was round and waxy-pale. The swelling came courtesy of steroids, the steroids courtesy of everything that was wrong with her. Her eyes, a brilliant green, shone like bits of costume jewelry poked into an uncooked loaf of bread. Her legs and arms were white sticks.
paragraphs.zh: 他走出来时，头戴的祈福小帽（kippah）微微偏斜，怀里抱着迪娜。她如果再长大一点，这个女孩就会对他来说太重了，但她会长到多高、会长多久这两个问题都没有明确答案。那是一个阴沉的万圣节前夕，她到底几岁？我不知道。她可能六岁，也可能十岁或十二岁。她的肚子在一件印有“I ❤ FLORIDA”的船领短衣下鼓了起来，脸又圆又蜡黄。肿胀是激素引起的，而这些激素又是因为她身体里的种种问题。她那双明亮的绿眼睛像是被插进未经烘烤面包里的小块假宝石，闪闪发亮。她的腿和胳膊像白色的细棒子。

paragraphs.en: From the May 2011 issue: Stephen King’s short story “Herman Wouk Is Still Alive”
paragraphs.zh: 选自2011年5月刊：斯蒂芬·金的短篇小说《赫曼·沃克仍然活着》。

paragraphs.en: Morris plopped her into her chair with a grunt of relief and pulled out the sunshade. Even with clouds, that UV is still a mother. From the stroller’s back pocket he took a bright-red hat with a wide, floppy brim. On the front it said RIOT GRRRL. That kiddo was the furthest thing from a punk rocker, but I loved the sentiment and I loved the hat and I liked Morris for taking good care of her. He wasn’t her father. I don’t know what their relationship was, just that there were no suspicious cuts or bruises on her. He kept her clean. That was good enough for me.
paragraphs.zh: 莫里斯把她一把放进椅子里，带着松一口气的轻哼，随后撑起了遮阳伞。即便阴天，紫外线也依然狠毒。从婴儿车后袋里，他拿出一顶鲜红色、帽沿宽大且边缘下垂的帽子。帽前绣着“RIOT GRRRL”。这孩子离朋克摇滚手最远了，但我喜欢这句话，我喜欢这顶帽子，也喜欢莫里斯对她细心照顾。那他并不是她的父亲。我不知道他们是什么关系，只知道她身上没有可疑的刀伤或淤伤。他把她打理得很干净，这对我来说已经够了。

paragraphs.en: “Sher-REE!” he shouted in falsetto, like Frankie Valli. He’d finished adjusting Dinah’s hat. Between it and the sunshade, her face was in deep shadow. “How goes your life in our Happy Haven?”
paragraphs.zh: “谢-瑞！”他用假声喊道，像弗兰基·瓦利那样。她的帽子和遮阳伞之间，她的脸都陷进了浓重阴影里。“你在我们的‘幸福港湾’过得怎么样？”

paragraphs.en: “Not bad,” I said, “but I wish those kids down there would move on to a different song. Even KC and the Sunshine Band would do.”
paragraphs.zh: “还行，”我说，“但我真希望那边那群孩子能换一首歌，哪怕换成《KC and the Sunshine Band》的歌也行。”

paragraphs.en: He lifted Dinah’s feet to display her plastic sandals. “Boogie shoes,” he said. “Come with us. We’ll urge them to change their tunes.”
paragraphs.zh: 他抬起迪娜的脚来给我看她的塑料凉鞋。“舞鞋，”他笑着说。“和我们一起来。我们会劝他们改换曲目。”

paragraphs.en: I had a doubt even then about whether or not that was a good idea, but I joined them. More fool me. “I don’t suppose you know a seven-letter word for a Thai tidbit? Or a river in Connecticut, last two letters I-C?”
paragraphs.zh: 当时我就怀疑这主意是否明智，但还是跟着他们一起去了。真是自作聪明。“你不会知道有个七个字母的词叫‘泰国小零嘴’吧？或者康涅狄格州的一条河流，最后两个字母是 I-C 吗？”

paragraphs.en: Dinah looked up at me from beneath her red hat. “Shah-ree.” She smiled, showing her three remaining molars. They were leaning like old gravestones. I thought the tooth fairy had gotten the best and would soon have the rest.
paragraphs.zh: 迪娜从她那顶红帽子底下抬头看着我。“Shah-ree。”她笑了，露出仅剩的三颗臼齿。它们歪得像古老的墓碑。我心想，牙仙子一定把最好的那颗先拿走了，其他的也很快会被她带走。

paragraphs.en: I took a knee—not easy when you’re a heavyweight, but I wanted to get down to her level. “How are you, pretty one?”
paragraphs.zh: 我跪了下来——对于一个体重很重的人来说并不容易，但我想和她在同一水平线上。“你好吗，漂亮宝贝？”

paragraphs.en: “Goo!” Dinah smiled more widely than ever. “I goo!”
paragraphs.zh: “Goo！”迪娜比平时笑得更开怀。“我也是，Goo！”

paragraphs.en: I raised one of her swollen hands and kissed it. “Great. I goo too.”
paragraphs.zh: 我扶起她那只肿胀的小手并轻轻亲了亲。“太好了，我也很好。”

paragraphs.en: “We … two … goo!” Dinah said, then laughed. She had a good one.
paragraphs.zh: “我们……两个……都很好！”迪娜说着笑了起来。她懂得开得很好。

paragraphs.en: “Your Connecticut river is ‘Niantic,’ ” Morris said. “Which makes Thai tidbits ‘ant eggs.’ ”
paragraphs.zh: “你们康涅狄格的河叫‘尼安廷克’，这样一来，泰式小吃就叫‘蚂蚁蛋’了。”莫里斯说。

paragraphs.en: Dinah made a face. It’s hard to know how much she understood, but she clearly found the idea of eating ant eggs disgusting.
paragraphs.zh: 黛娜做了个鬼脸。她到底理解了多少谁也说不准，但她显然觉得“吃蚂蚁蛋”这个想法很恶心。

paragraphs.en: “Thanks! You’re a wonder, Morris.”
paragraphs.zh: “谢谢！你真是个奇才，莫里斯。”

paragraphs.en: He shrugged. “I’ve done beaucoup crosswords in my day. I’m wise to their tricks. Walk with us?”
paragraphs.zh: 他耸了耸肩：“我这辈子做过很多填字游戏，懂它们的套路了。要和我们一起走吗？”

paragraphs.en: “All right.” I filled in the missing letters of the head-scratchers and put the magazine on my cinder-block step. The few remaining clues looked easy. “Let’s go. The beach, I suppose?”
paragraphs.zh: “好吧。”我把这些令人抓脑的填字题空缺补好了，并把杂志放在我的混凝土砖台阶上。剩下的几个线索看起来很容易。“我们走吧。去海边吧？”

paragraphs.en: “Yeah,” Morris said. “She likes to look at the water. And the birds.”
paragraphs.zh: “是啊，”莫里斯说。“她喜欢看水，也喜欢看鸟。”

paragraphs.en: “Wah-wah burts!” Dinah said.
paragraphs.zh: “哇——哇——咕！”迪娜说。

paragraphs.en: “Water and birds, that is correct, mademoiselle. Off we go.”
paragraphs.zh: “看水和鸟，没错，小姐。我们走了。”

paragraphs.en: The few street names in Happy Haven were determinedly upbeat, with Christian undertones. Hallelujah was the main thoroughfare. Crossing it were Redemption Street, Cheery Close, and Joyful Boulevard. Mostly we were cheerful (if not always joyful), but there were apt to be raised voices after drunk o’clock, when the Dead River Bar closed and the local bikers roared back down Highway 41. Sometimes it was the Harrigans arguing in their Aliner. Sometimes the Sanchezes in their fancy-schmancy Forest River. Once or twice there were gunshots, but that’s not unusual in Florida, and nobody has ever been killed, at least to my knowledge, although Mitch Yellin shot himself in the leg two years ago practicing his quick-draw move in his backyard. Mostly we’re all right. When there’s trouble, it has a tendency to come from the water.
paragraphs.zh: Happy Haven 几乎每条街名都带着刻意的阳光意味，带有基督教色彩。Hallelujah 是主干道。穿过它的是 Redemption Street、Cheery Close 和 Joyful Boulevard。大多数时候我们都很愉快（不一定总是快乐），但到了“醉酒时段”，当 Dead River Bar 关门、当地摩托帮沿 41 号公路哐啷哐啷驶回时，吵架声往往会提高音量。有时是 Harrigans 家在他们的 Aliner 里争吵；有时是 Sanchezes 家在他们那辆精致的 Forest River 里争执。有时还会传来枪声，但在佛罗里达这并不罕见，至少据我所知，没有人被打死，尽管 Mitch Yellin 两年前在后院练习快拔枪时打到了自己腿。大体上我们都过得不错。每当出事，麻烦往往都从水边冒出来。

paragraphs.en: I actually thought this as we strolled down Hallelujah Avenue toward the music. I should have listened.
paragraphs.zh: 当我们沿着 Hallelujah 大道向音乐声走去时，我其实就是这么想的。那时我应该听进去。

paragraphs.en: Dinah suffered from some kind of cancer, complicated by a stroke, liver damage, and erythropoietic protoporphyria. That rare malady results in an accumulation of the pigment found in red blood cells. It causes acute sensitivity to sunlight, which was why she was so pale and Morris took her out only on cloudy days. Those days aren’t all that common in Florida, which isn’t nicknamed the Sunshine State for nothing. It’s true that UV can mean a sunburn on cloudy days, but in bright sunshine, even with the shade over her stroller and her floppy RIOT GRRRL hat, Dinah would have cooked like a cheese sandwich in a microwave.
paragraphs.zh: 迪娜罹患了某种癌症，并并发了中风、肝损伤和红细胞原卟啉原病（erythropoietic protoporphyria）。这种罕见病会导致红细胞内色素堆积。它会引起对阳光的急性敏感，这也是她如此苍白的原因，所以 Morris 只在阴天带她出门。佛罗里达并不太常有阴天，这个州被叫作阳光之州也不是白叫的。确实，阴天也会被紫外线晒伤，但在强烈日光下，即便有伞布遮住她的婴儿车、她还戴着那顶蓬松的 RIOT GRRRL 帽子，迪娜也会在阳光里像奶酪三明治放进微波炉一样被烤化。

paragraphs.en: She could have lived a normal life with EPP if not for the cancer. That occasioned the steroids and probably caused the stroke. The liver damage might well have been caused by EPP, or maybe it came with the original equipment and was just waiting for the moment to jump out of its box and play its part in scrambling Dinah’s brains.
paragraphs.zh: 如果不是癌症，她本可以用 EPP 正常生活。这场癌症引发了激素治疗，并且很可能导致了中风。肝损伤很可能也是 EPP 所致，或者说它本来就和她的身体条件一并“出厂”了，只是在等待时机跳出来，参与到扰乱迪娜大脑的那一幕里。

paragraphs.en: Morris was closemouthed about her origins, but enumerated her various conditions with a kind of doleful enjoyment. He told me, after one of her frequent visits to Sarasota Memorial, that her condition was “on the border line.” I didn’t ask him what that meant, because I was pretty sure I knew. All I cared about was that, though Dinah bruised easily, Morris wasn’t the cause. He and I were neighbors and friends, but not what I’d call besties, and I didn’t want to get any closer to him and Dinah than I already was. I’d lost a few besties along the way, and I was not eager to lose another one. Or two.
paragraphs.zh: 莫里斯对她的身世守口如瓶，但却会带着一种忧郁又有点享受的语气，逐一列出她各类病症。那次他经常来往于萨拉索塔纪念医院后告诉我，丹娜的情况“处于边缘状态”。我并未追问是什么意思，因为我差不多知道了。我只在意一件事：虽然丹娜很容易受伤，罪魁祸首却不是莫里斯。他和我既是邻居也是朋友，但谈不上“最好的朋友”，而且我不想在我和他与丹娜之间再进一步靠近。一路走来我已经失去了几位最要好的朋友，我可不想再失去一个。或者两个。

paragraphs.en: The trailer park’s main drag was paved, but at the stake fence that marked its end, the asphalt turned to hardpan dirt. The music became louder. Still “Heavy Music.” The long version that’s on Seger’s ‘Live’ Bullet double album. The road went down a shallow grade to the beach. The brochure for our trailer park proclaims FULL BEACH ACCESS, and it doesn’t lie, but the beach is little more than a hundred-yard swatch of white sand with the boat landing on one side and the Sunset City condos on the other. It’s a nice beach for what it is … but it ain’t much. And right away my bad feeling doubled. No, tripled.
paragraphs.zh: 拖车营地的主干道是铺了柏油的，但在那道标记营地尽头的木桩篱笆处，沥青变成了硬化黏土。音乐越来越响。仍然是“重金属”。那首在西格的《Live》长版双专辑里的长曲。路向海滩缓缓下坡。我们营地的宣传册上明明写着“FULL BEACH ACCESS”，也没骗人，但海滩也就一百码左右的一片白沙，边上是船泊位，另一边是日落城的公寓。就它本身而言，这算是个不错的海滩……但也算不了什么。我的不安立刻加倍。不是加倍，是三倍。

paragraphs.en: See, half a dozen guys were down there, throwing around a big, old yellow Nerf football, kicking up sand, weaving around a couple of Styrofoam coolers, and tackling one another. A guy with a goatee yanked down another guy’s salmon-pink board shorts, and I got a better look at his lily-white butt than I wanted before he yanked them back up. These were not preppy tourist kids but townies with townie buzz cuts. No girls; it was strictly a stag party. Sometimes girls have a way of mellowing guys out. Not always, but sometimes.
paragraphs.zh: 你看，那里有六七个年轻男人，在那里扔着一个又大又旧的黄色 Nerf 足球，踢起沙子，绕着两个发泡塑料冷藏箱穿行，互相把对方拦腰抱倒。一个留山羊胡的男人把另一个男人的鲑鱼粉泳裤扯了下来，我在他把泳裤扯回去之前，得以看清那人的雪白臀部，远远比我愿意看的。这里的不是穿得花枝招展、像游客那样的“预备生”小孩，而是本地男孩，像本地人那样的平头。没有女孩；这是一次纯男士聚会。女生有时会让男人缓和些。不是总是，但有时候会。

paragraphs.en: That wasn’t the worst part, though. Drunk young studs are common as dirt on the Suncoast. The worst was the party boat they’d arrived in. I recognized from the blue and red pinstripes that it was one of the rentals from Cool Water Mama, in Nokomis. This boat’s party days were over. The kid piloting it, no doubt drunk as a skunk, hadn’t bothered anchoring, just drove it up onto the beach, where it now leaned askew, one of its pontoons dented and the other torn off and bobbing four feet out.
paragraphs.zh: 但那还不是最糟的。阳光海岸上醉酒的年轻壮汉多得像尘土。最糟的是他们乘来的派对船。我从那艘船的蓝红条纹认出，它是诺科米斯“酷水妈妈”出租的一种。这个船的狂欢时代已经结束。那个开船的孩子，毫无疑问是烂醉如泥，没费心抛锚，而是直接把船开上了沙滩，如今它斜斜地倚在那儿，一个浮筒被撞凹了，另一个被扯掉了，漂在外面四英尺高。

paragraphs.en: The kids didn’t seem to know or care that when they got back—on foot—they’d be looking at a $2,000 deductible. If, that was, they’d taken the insurance, and I guessed they’d been too high to bother. You could blame Skip Kilgallon for renting to them in the first place, but I had to give him a break on that. Until the season gets going after the year-end holidays, everyone on the Gulf is scraping by.
paragraphs.zh: 那些孩子看起来既不知道也不在乎——等他们走回去步行时，还要面对两千美元的免赔额。前提是他们买过保险，而我猜他们当时可能觉得太贵了，所以也没买。要怪也得怪斯基普·基尔甘先把船租给了他们，但我得给他个台阶下。在年末假期之后季节尚未真正开工前，海湾那边的人基本都在勉强维持生计。

paragraphs.en: The speakers on the cabin of the gravely wounded pontoon boat were momentarily silent, then once more began to blast “Heavy Music.” There were yells of bro and dude.
paragraphs.zh: 受重伤的充气驳船船舱里的音响先是短暂地沉默了一下，接着又开始大声播放《Heavy Music》。周围响起了“bro”和“dude”的呼喊。

paragraphs.en: “You know what,” I said, catching Morris’s arm, “this might not be such a good idea.”
paragraphs.zh: “你知道吗，”我抓住莫里斯的手臂说，“这主意恐怕不太妙。”

paragraphs.en: “Burts!” Dinah said, and pointed toward the water. Under the thinning clouds, the Gulf was like a fogged mirror. “Burts!”
paragraphs.zh: “伯茨！”迪娜说着，指向水面。在云层渐淡的上空，海湾像一面起了雾的镜子。“伯茨！”

paragraphs.en: “No, it’ll be fine,” Morris said. “They are just having a good time.”
paragraphs.zh: “不会的，没事的，”莫里斯说，“他们只是玩得很开心。”

paragraphs.en: “Such a good time that they wrecked one of Skip Kilgallon’s pontoon boats,” I said.
paragraphs.zh: “开心得把斯基普·基尔冈的一艘浮筒船都搞坏了。”我说。

paragraphs.en: It was like he hadn’t heard me. “And everybody likes Dinah. What’s not to like?” That was true, most people did like Dinah, and made much of her. Coochie-coo and all that. She wasn’t pretty, and of course she was mentally disabled—had the mind and vocabulary of a toddler—but even so, she had a certain charm. Joie de vivre? Maybe. Morris liked to show her off. He also enjoyed the kudos he got for taking good care of her. So down the hill we went, into that throbbing, repetitive bass line and into trouble.
paragraphs.zh: 他似乎没听见我说话。“而且每个人都喜欢迪娜，哪有什么不对？”这倒也没错，大多数人都喜欢迪娜，也都很重视她，喜欢去逗她、哄她、夸她。她不漂亮，而且她当然有精神发育障碍——思维和词汇量都像个学步幼儿——但她仍有一种特别的魅力。也许就是那种对生活的热情？莫里斯喜欢把她拿出来炫耀；他也很享受别人因此给他一个好照顾她的好名声。于是我们下了山，走进那一遍遍跳动、反复不休的低音鼓点里，也就踩上了麻烦。

paragraphs.en: A blond kid, shirtless, tall, built like a brick outhouse, was the first to see the stroller and the oversize girl in it. He grabbed a beer from one of the coolers and kangaroo-leaped over to us, kicking up sand and shouting, “Hey, baby! Hey, baby!”
paragraphs.zh: 一个金发男孩，第一个发现了童车和里面那个身形很大的女孩。他从一个保冷箱里抓起一罐啤酒，像袋鼠一样一跳一跳地冲过来，扬起一片沙子，大喊：“嘿，宝贝！嘿，宝贝！”

paragraphs.en: Dinah gave her charming (mostly toothless) grin and pointed past him. “Burts! See burts!”
paragraphs.zh: 迪娜露出她迷人的笑容（几乎全是缺牙），指着他身后说：“Burts！看见了没，Burts！”

paragraphs.en: A tubby boy ran up, catching the Nerf football over his shoulder and falling on his knees, spraying sand onto Dinah’s lap. He stared at her as if at an exhibit. “ ‘Burts’ is right! ‘Burts’ and Ernies! We’re goin’ to Sesame Street!”
paragraphs.zh: 一个圆滚滚的男孩跑了过来，脖子一甩接住那只“Nerf”足球，随即跪倒在地，沙子飞溅到迪娜的腿上。

paragraphs.en: The others clustered around, circling the stroller and throwing shade. The smell of beer and pot was strong.
paragraphs.zh: 其他人也围了过去，围着婴儿车挤成一圈，对她指指点点。酒精和大麻的味道很重。

paragraphs.en: “What’s wrong with her?” one of them asked. He had a cat tattoo on one bicep. “Is she sick, or what?”
paragraphs.zh: “她怎么了？”其中一个人问道。他的二头肌上纹着一只猫。“她病了吗，还是怎么了？”

paragraphs.en: “She is quite sick,” Morris said in a lecturely voice. “She has erythropoietic proto—”
paragraphs.zh: “她确实病得很重，”Morris用讲课般的腔调说。“她得了红细胞生成性——”

paragraphs.en: “She’s a alien!” another shouted. This one had a mohawk. He bent over, hands on sandy knees. Studied her and said: “A retarded alien. Are you a retarded alien, honey? Need to phone home?”
paragraphs.zh: “她是个外星人！”另一个人喊道。那个人留着莫霍克发型。他弯下腰，双手搭在沙色的膝盖上。打量着她说：“一个智障外星人。你是个智障外星人吗，亲爱的？要不要打个电话回家？”

paragraphs.en: “That’s not a word we use,” Morris said, but Dinah smiled uncertainly.
paragraphs.zh: “这是我们不用的说法，”Morris说，但Dinah不确定地笑了笑。

paragraphs.en: The boys were crowding me out, so I elbowed back in. “Morris, I think we should—”
paragraphs.zh: 那些男孩把我挤到外面，我就把他们肘开了。“Morris，我想我们应该——”

paragraphs.en: One of the kids, the boy with the trying-too-hard goatee, snatched off Morris’s kippah. “Yid lid, Yid lid!” He spun it in the air. Morris lunged for it and missed. Goatee tossed it to Blondie and the boys spread out, throwing it around in a circle. They all started to chant “Yid lid.” Dinah didn’t like all the yelling. She started to cry.
paragraphs.zh: 有个男孩——那个把三分像样胡子扎得很“努力看起来很酷”的孩子，抢下了莫里斯的黑边小礼帽（kippah），“Yid lid，Yid lid！”他把帽子抛在空中。莫里斯猛扑过去想接住，却没接上。那胡子男把帽子扔给了布朗迪，几个男孩就这样分散开，在一个圆圈里把它一人传一人。大家都开始齐声喊“Yid lid”。狄娜不喜欢这种喧闹，她开始哭了。

paragraphs.en: I was pissed. Mostly at them, but also at Morris. He had wanted to come among this pride of young and drunk lions to show off his darling Dinah. He’d been expecting a lot of cooing and positive strokes, like when he pushed Dinah’s chair into the circle of ladies who formed the Mystery-Book Club. He had ignored the warning signs, most especially the ruined party boat.
paragraphs.zh: 我当时很生气。主要是对他们，也有一点对莫里斯的气。他本想混进这群年轻又半醉的“雄狮”里，趁机炫耀自己可爱的狄娜。像他把狄娜的椅子推到组成“神秘书会”的女士圆圈时那样，他本想得到一片柔情的哄声和赞许的抚慰。可他忽视了所有警告信号，尤其是那艘坏掉的派对船。

paragraphs.en: “Give it back!” I shouted. “Give it back, you fuckheads!”
paragraphs.zh: “还回去！”我喊道。“还回去，你们这些王八蛋！”

paragraphs.en: The boy in the pink board shorts asked me if I kissed my mother with that mouth. The rest of them laughed.
paragraphs.zh: 穿粉红色泳裤的那个男孩问我：‘你是用那张嘴和你妈妈接吻的吗？’其余人都笑了。

paragraphs.en: The afternoon was brightening, the sun preparing to come through. If it did, it was going to be tough on Dinah. Just one more delight in what was turning out to be a delightful fucking day. The six of them weren’t out of control, but dancing on the edge of it. The boat, I thought. They either don’t realize what they did to it, or don’t care. Morris, what other warning did you need?
paragraphs.zh: 下午光线正转亮，太阳正准备穿透云层。如果真露出来，对黛娜来说会很艰难。仅仅是这一天正变得如此美好时，又添了一种痛快但糟糕的刺激。那六个人并未完全失控，却在失控的边缘上起舞。那条船，我心里想。他们要么没意识到自己把船搞成了什么样，要么才不在乎。莫里斯，你还需要什么别的警告？

paragraphs.en: One of the boys spun the kippah to the blond boy. Morris leaped for it, his face a mask of outrage.
paragraphs.zh: 其中一个男孩把基帕（犹太礼帽）甩给那个金发男孩。莫里斯立刻扑了过去，脸上挂着一副义愤填膺的面孔。

paragraphs.en: “Look out, Morrie!” I shouted, but too late. His sandal-clad foot came down on the stroller and knocked it on its side. Dinah spilled out, at first too startled to cry. Morris fell on top of her, knees spread to keep from squashing Dinah’s chest and maybe killing her.
paragraphs.zh: “小心点，莫里！”我大喊，但已经晚了。他那只穿凉鞋的脚踩在婴儿车上，把它掀翻了。黛娜一下子蹦了出来，起初吓得连哭都来不及。莫里斯扑在她身上，双膝张开，尽量不把黛娜的胸口压碎，免得把她弄坏。

paragraphs.en: “Check it out!” Goatee yelled. “Yid boy’s dry-humpin’ his idjit daughter! Somebody call ICE!”
paragraphs.zh: “看见了吗！”那个络腮胡叫道，“那个犹太小子正在对他那傻姑娘做干而不入式的下流动作！快去叫 ICE！”

paragraphs.en: Dinah’s floppy RIOT GRRRL hat had come off. One of the boys picked it up and threw it like a Frisbee. Tattoo Boy caught it, then spun it to Mohawk Boy. They were drifting down toward the water, the kippah forgotten in the sand.
paragraphs.zh: 迪娜那顶宽边的 RIOT GRRRL 帽子掉了下来。一个男孩捡起来像飞盘一样扔了出去。纹身男孩接住了它，然后又扔给了莫霍克男孩。他们正飘向水边，kippah 被落在了沙地里。

paragraphs.en: “We have to get her out of the sun,” Morris said.
paragraphs.zh: “我们得把她移到太阳底下去。”莫里斯说。

paragraphs.en: It was still only a white disk above the thinning clouds, but the skin on her cheeks was red and a blister was forming over her right eye. The sunshade on her stroller had gotten twisted around its thin chrome bars, reminding me of the pontoon that had been torn off.
paragraphs.zh: 天上只剩下薄云后的一轮白色圆盘，但她脸颊上的皮肤已经发红，右眼上正在长起一个水泡。她婴儿车上的遮阳篷扭进了细小的铬杆之间，让我想起被扯离的浮筒。

paragraphs.en: “Sherry, little help here!”
paragraphs.zh: “雪莉，来帮帮忙！”

paragraphs.en: He picked Dinah up and settled her into the stroller. One wheel sank in the sand and she fell out again. She stopped crying, and that scared me more than anything.
paragraphs.zh: 他把黛娜抱起来，安顿进婴儿车。一个车轮陷进了沙里，她又摔了出来。她停止哭泣了，而这让我比任何事都更害怕。

paragraphs.en: “Stand back,” I said, and picked Dinah up. “Get the stroller out of the sand. Hurry up.”
paragraphs.zh: “先退后一点，”我说着，把黛娜抱了起来。“把婴儿车从沙里拖出来，快点。”

paragraphs.en: The girl was surprisingly heavy. Sand had stuck to the mucus from her nose, giving her a mustache. The first blister had been joined by another.
paragraphs.zh: 那个女孩出乎意料地重。沙子粘在她鼻涕上，让她看起来像留了胡子。第一个水疱已经合并成第二个。

paragraphs.en: Except for Blondie, the boys were now ignoring us. Which was fine with me. The one with the mohawk curled his arm and scaled Dinah’s hat high into the air. The wind caught it and it sailed over the water, landing beside the half-submerged pontoon, which would never float again. Incredibly, everyone except the blond kid was laughing. He at least had the good grace to look worried. The maddening bass signature of “Heavy Music” played over and over.
paragraphs.zh: 除了布朗妮，男孩子们现在都在无视我们。对我来说没关系。那个留着莫霍克头的男孩弯起手臂，把黛娜的帽子高高掷了起来。风把它拽走了，帽子飞过水面，落在半淹没的浮筒旁边，再也浮不起来了。难以置信的是，除了那个金发男孩，其他人都在大笑。至少他还算有礼貌地露出一副担忧的样子。那首反复响起、让人烦躁的低音主旋律《Heavy Music》一遍又一遍地在播放。

paragraphs.en: Morris yanked the stroller out of the sand and I put Dinah in it. I will never forget her bulging eyes, so green, like sea glass. She knew something bad was happening but not what it was. Her scant hair was full of sand. I carried the stroller to the edge of the hardpan road that boaters used to back their small craft down to the edge of the water. Later that night I would have trouble bending over and taking off my sneakers, but right then, muscle memory—and panic, that too—had me feeling 19 again. Once her stroller was clear of the sand, I stripped off my shirt and billowed it over the stroller like a tent.
paragraphs.zh: 莫里斯把婴儿车从沙里拖了出来，我把迪娜放进里面。我永远不会忘记她那双突出的大眼睛，绿得像海玻璃。她知道有不好的事情要发生了，却说不清到底是什么。她的稀薄发丝全是沙子。我把婴儿车拖到小船夫习惯将小艇往水边开（倒）去的硬化路边。那天晚上我弯腰脱运动鞋很吃力，但那一刻，肌肉记忆——还有恐慌——让我又觉得自己回到了十九岁。婴儿车一离开沙子，我就脱掉上衣，像搭帐篷一样披在婴儿车上。

paragraphs.en: “Holy shit, look at them jahoobies!” Goatee Boy called. He was handsome. He could have been a model in a catalog. “Are those 44s or 48s?”
paragraphs.zh: “我的天，看看那对奶子吧！”山羊胡男叫道。他长得很帅，像宣传册里的模特一样。“那是44D还是48D？”

paragraphs.en: “Forty-eight D’s!” yelled the short, tubby one. He had a PBR in one sandy fist. “Those ain’t tits, they’re cannons! Anti-aircraft shells! Where’d you get a bra to hold ’em? Boobs R Us?”
paragraphs.zh: “48D！”矮乎乎、圆乎乎的那个人喊道。他一只满是沙子的手里拿着一罐PBR。“那可不是奶子，那是大炮！是防空炮弹！你从哪儿弄来一件能托住它们的胸罩？‘Boobs R Us’？”

paragraphs.en: Morris was looking dazedly at his kippah. He put it on his head. Sand fell out and ran down the sides of his face. The boys howled. I suppose it was funny, in a slapstick sort of way. Even Blondie was smiling, although he seemed to have some idea that the fun had gone too far.
paragraphs.zh: 莫里斯怔怔地盯着自己的小礼帽（kippah）。他把它戴在头上，沙子从中掉出来，顺着脸两侧往下流。那些男孩大笑不止。我想，大概还真有点喜剧效果，像默默剧那样夸张滑稽。连布朗迪都在笑，虽然他似乎有点意识到这玩笑已经太过分了。

paragraphs.en: “Morris!” I yelled. “Morris, come on!” He looked around at me as if startled out of a dream.
paragraphs.zh: “莫里斯！”我大喊。“莫里斯，快点来！”他像是被梦里惊醒一样，看着我四处张望。

paragraphs.en: The blond boy came toward me, bumping Morris with one shoulder and almost knocking him over again.
paragraphs.zh: 那个金发男孩朝我走了过来，用一个肩膀撞了下莫里斯，差点又把他推倒。

paragraphs.en: “Listen,” Blondie said.
paragraphs.zh: “听着，”金发男孩说。

paragraphs.en: I held up my hand like a traffic cop. “Don’t fucking touch me.”
paragraphs.zh: 我像交警一样举起手。“别他妈碰我。”

paragraphs.en: “Fuck off!” I yelled, and he backed away.
paragraphs.zh: “滚开！”我喊道，他便退了回来。

paragraphs.en: Tattoo Boy was wading out to get Dinah’s hat. He grabbed it, waved it over his head, and whooped. The sun came out. Water droplets flew off the brim. I remember that. It’s clear, like a photograph.
paragraphs.zh: “纹身男孩”跋涉到水里去拿迪娜的帽子。他抓住它，举过头顶，欢呼起来。太阳露了出来，水珠从帽檐上飞溅出来。我记得那一幕，清晰得像一张照片。

paragraphs.en: Under my shirt, Dinah was crying again.
paragraphs.zh: 我胸口的衣服里，迪娜又开始哭了。

paragraphs.en: “Morris! Morris, help me!”
paragraphs.zh: “莫里斯！莫里斯，帮我！”

paragraphs.en: “I don’t give a flying fuck about her hat!”
paragraphs.zh: “我他妈才不在乎她那顶帽子！”

paragraphs.en: We pushed her back up the hill, leaving the boys on the beach. The music cut out, creating a hole, one that was somehow as loud as the Silver Bullet Band in full flight. Pushing was hard because one of the stroller’s wheels was now crooked. Dinah had gone silent under the makeshift tent. That quiet felt ominous to me. Like the sudden lack of “Heavy Music.”
paragraphs.zh: 我们把她推回到小坡上，留下男孩们在沙滩上。音乐忽然停了，像挖出一个洞，这种空白反而异常响亮，仿佛有一支全力开火的 Silver Bullet Band 在演奏。推车很费劲，因为童车的一只轮子已经歪了。迪娜在简易帐篷下沉默了。那种沉默让我觉得不祥。就像“Heavy Music”突然消失那样。

paragraphs.en: We were back where Hallelujah Avenue began. Except for Blondie, the boys were facing away from us, looking at the listing pontoon boat as if they were just realizing they were going to be in a world of shit when they reported back to Skip.
paragraphs.zh: 我们回到了哈利路亚大道的起点。除了布朗迪，男孩们都背对着我们，盯着那艘向一侧倾斜的驳船，仿佛他们才刚意识到，等会儿向Skip汇报后会陷入一场烂摊子。

paragraphs.en: The blond boy raised his hand tentatively. I gave him the finger, and there was nothing tentative about it.
paragraphs.zh: 金发男孩试探性地抬起了手。我冲他竖了中指，毫不含糊。

paragraphs.en: Morris and I had been neighbors for eight months or more, but I’d been in his Airstream only a few times—very few. (Not besties, remember?) It was small and dark and cramped. There were two chairs facing the TV, one big and plump, the other small and plump. They looked like garage-sale specials. I had carried Dinah in because Morris wasn’t up to it. He sat in the big chair. Collapsed in it, more like, gasping for breath. I put Dinah in the small one and leaned on the tiny counter in the kitchenette, catching my own breath. Dinah was making a sound somewhere between a sob and a grizzle. “Bat boys,” she said, and I assumed she wasn’t talking about boys who were also superheroes.
paragraphs.zh: 我和莫里斯已经做了八个多月邻居，但我只去过他那辆艾斯特里姆拖车几次——真的很少。（你记得吧，我们不是闺蜜。）那地方又小又暗、又挤。里面有两把朝电视的椅子，一把又大又圆润，另一把也圆圆的但小一点，看起来像二手车库甩卖才有的破家具。我把黛娜抱了进去，因为莫里斯体力不支，没法抬。莫里斯坐在大椅子上，准确地说，是瘫在上面，喘着气。我把黛娜放到小椅子上，然后靠在小厨房的台面上，自己也先慢慢喘口气。黛娜发出一种介于呜咽和咕哝之间的声音。她说：“蝙蝠男孩。”我就猜她说的不是也会当超级英雄的男孩。

paragraphs.en: “Give me your phone,” I said. “I’ll call 911.”
paragraphs.zh: 我说：“把手机给我，我去打911。”

paragraphs.en: “No,” Morris said. “She’s better. Aren’t you, precious?”
paragraphs.zh: 莫里斯说：“不，”黛娜听着更好点了，是不是，亲爱的？”

paragraphs.en: “Bet,” Dinah agreed. “Bat boys.”
paragraphs.zh: “好呀，”黛娜附和道。“蝙蝠男孩。”

paragraphs.en: “They were,” Morris said, and tried a smile. One thing I could say for Morris: He always tried to look on life’s sunny side. “Probably they’re sorry now.”
paragraphs.zh: “是他们，”莫里斯说着，勉强露出一丝笑容。我想说莫里斯有一处值得称道：他总是尽量从生活里看到阳光的一面。“他们现在可能已经后悔了。”

paragraphs.en: “Sorry,” Dinah said, then pointed at me. “Sherry!”
paragraphs.zh: “对不起，”迪娜说着，然后指着我说：“雪莉！”

paragraphs.en: “You got it, cabbage,” I said. “You got the head on ya.” Then, to Morris: “We should take her to urgent care, at least. There’s one on Bee Ridge.”
paragraphs.zh: “你猜对了，宝贝，”我说。“你是被打到头了。”然后对莫里斯说：“我们至少应该把她送去急诊。这附近在 Bee Ridge 有一家。”

paragraphs.en: “No need. She’ll bounce back. She’s gotten sun before and she always gets better.”
paragraphs.zh: “没必要。她很快就会好起来。她以前也被晒过太阳，而且总是很快恢复。”

paragraphs.en: Sun was the least of her problems, I thought, but she did look a little better. “At least let me clean her up.”
paragraphs.zh: 我想，太阳只是她烦恼里最不严重的一项，但她看起来确实好了一点。“至少让我替她整理打理一下吧。”

paragraphs.en: His bathroom was the size of a telephone booth. It contained a sink, a toilet, and a shelf crammed with Dinah’s prescriptions. There was no shower; he probably took care of his daily ablutions in Happy Haven’s Courtesy Center. A damp washcloth was in the tiny sink, but it didn’t smell mildewy. Morris wasn’t the worst male housekeeper I’ve ever seen, but like most of them, Mama will take care of it was probably always somewhere in the back of his mind. I wrung out the washcloth and wiped sand and snot off Dinah’s cheeks and chin.
paragraphs.zh: 他的浴室大概有个小电话亭那么大，里面有一个洗手盆、一个马桶，以及一层塞满蒂娜处方药的架子。没有淋浴间；他大概是在快乐港的礼貌中心里完成日常清洁的。一个潮湿的毛巾放在那只小洗手盆里，但没有发霉的味道。莫里斯算不上我见过最差的男性管家，但和大多数人一样，“妈妈会来处理”这种想法恐怕一直在他脑后盘旋着。我把毛巾拧干后，擦掉了蒂娜脸颊和下巴上的沙子和鼻涕。

paragraphs.en: “Does that feel good?”
paragraphs.zh: “这样舒服吗？”

paragraphs.en: “Yes, I bet it does. Give me a smile, can you?”
paragraphs.zh: “当然，肯定舒服。给我笑一个，好吗？”

paragraphs.en: She did, showing her nearly toothless gums. She couldn’t chew. When Morris fed her, it was mostly yogurt, soup, and Gerber dinners. Despite the nubs under her long-sleeved shirt that would one day be breasts (assuming she lived long enough), she was little more than a baby; childhood strokes are the gift that keeps on giving.
paragraphs.zh: 她照做了，嘴里露出几乎见不到牙齿的牙龈。她已经咬不动东西了。莫里斯给她喂食时，大多是酸奶、汤和嘉宝辅食。尽管在她长袖衬衫下方已经有了将来会变成胸部的突起（前提是她活得够久），她仍然几乎还是个婴儿；童年期的拥抱和抚摸，确实是受益终生的礼物。

paragraphs.en: “You still should get her checked out,” I said. “I’ll drive you, if you feel like you can’t—”
paragraphs.zh: “你还是应该带她去看一下，”我说，“如果你觉得自己开不了车，我可以送你去。”

paragraphs.en: “No,” he said again. Then, as if it were a great realization: “We should never have gone down there.”
paragraphs.zh: “不，”他又说了一遍。接着仿佛有了一个巨大的领悟：“我们当初根本不该去那边。”

paragraphs.en: With a mighty effort, I restrained myself from saying, “I told you so.”
paragraphs.zh: 我用了很大的力气，才忍住不说：“我早就告诉过你。”

paragraphs.en: Give him credit: He said it for me. “You told me that. I didn’t listen.”
paragraphs.zh: 这要表扬他：这句话是他替我说出来的。“你当时告诉过我，但我没听进去。”

paragraphs.en: Dinah’s eyes closed. Her head slipped against her shoulder as she sat in her chair, reminding me of the pontoon boat, sitting beached and askew.
paragraphs.zh: 迪娜闭上眼睛。她坐在椅子上，头靠在肩上侧着身子，让我想起那艘搁浅且歪斜停着的浮桥船。

paragraphs.en: “She’s going to sleep,” I said.
paragraphs.zh: “她要睡着了。”我说。

paragraphs.en: “Already gone. Best thing for her. Knits up the raveled sleeve of care.” He smiled. “I think I saw that in a Sominex ad.”
paragraphs.zh: “已经睡着了。对她来说这才是最好的，能把操心事像毛边被磨损的袖口一样一点点缝好。”他笑了笑。“我想我在苏米奈克斯的广告里见过这句。”

paragraphs.en: “When you get around to taking her to the Courtesy Center, be sure to wash her hair.”
paragraphs.zh: “等你有空把她带去礼貌中心时，一定要给她洗头。”

paragraphs.en: I realized I didn’t know if he showered with her. She could stand and walk a little (although it was more like lurching), but I had never seen her run like a normal child. Because—duh—she wasn’t normal. Could she wash herself? What would he do when she got her period? Could she get her period? What were they to each other, anyway? Did I want to know the backstory, or was that too far down the road to besties?
paragraphs.zh: 我这才意识到自己不知道他是否和她一起洗澡。她能站起来并走一会儿（虽然更像是摇摇晃晃地踉跄着），但我从没见过她像正常孩子那样跑步。因为——显然——她不正常。她能自己洗澡吗？他在她来月经时会怎么办？她能来月经吗？反正他们之间到底是什么关系？我想知道她们的过去吗，还是这已经是谈“好朋友”之前必须跨过的一道太远的界线？

paragraphs.en: “I could go to Frankie’s,” I said. “Could you eat something, Morris?”
paragraphs.zh: “我可以去弗兰基那里，”我说，“Morris，你能先吃点东西吗？”

paragraphs.en: “Sure,” he said. “A tuna-fish sandwich would be great. Mushrooms and lots of black olives. What do you want?”
paragraphs.zh: “当然，”他说。“一份金枪鱼三明治就很好了。要放蘑菇和很多黑橄榄。你想要什么？”

paragraphs.en: “Pizza gives me the runs,” Morris said. “I think it’s the oregano.”
paragraphs.zh: “披萨会让我腹泻，”莫里斯说。“我想是欧芹草（牛至）惹的祸。”

paragraphs.en: “TMI, Morris. Isn’t that what the kids say?”
paragraphs.zh: “太过了，莫里斯。不是只有孩子才会这么说吗？”

paragraphs.en: “If I ever was one, it’s too far back to remember. Happy to buy. There’s money under the book on my night table.”
paragraphs.zh: “如果我以前真有这种毛病，也早到记不清楚了。愿意买单。我床头柜上放书的地方下面有钱。”

paragraphs.en: I thought of saying we’d go dutch, but I felt the first warning twinges in my back and decided that since he was the besotted fool who’d wanted to take Dinah down to the beach and show her off, he could pay for our dinner.
paragraphs.zh: 我本想说我们AA（AA制），但我背后忽然一阵预警似的刺痛让我警觉，于是想，如果是他这个迷恋迪娜、想把她带去海边炫耀的傻乎乎的人，那他就应该付我们的晚餐。”

paragraphs.en: There was well over $200 in 20s under a book called Raising Children Right. Thumbing through his stash, I realized I also had no idea where Morris got his dough. The Suncoast is pretty and its climate is temperate, but it’s not cheap. Lot rental alone at Happy Haven is almost $900 a month.
paragraphs.zh: 在一本叫《Raising Children Right》的书下面，放着超过200美元，都是二十美元纸币。我翻看自己这点存货时，也意识到我根本不知道莫里斯那笔钱是从哪里来的。阳光海岸风景很美，气候也很温和，但并不便宜。Happy Haven 的车位租金一个月就快900美元。

paragraphs.en: “Going,” I said. “Back soon.”
paragraphs.zh: 我说：“去一趟。” “很快就回来。”

paragraphs.en: I called in the take-out order in my perfect-for-one Scamp trailer and had a wash. I picked the food up at Frankie’s in my Neon. Dinah looked better when I got back. She was wearing a bib (I’M A BIG GIRL NOW) and feeding herself some kind of beige baby goo. She was also wearing a good deal of it. She raised one hand in a cheery wave when I came in. “Sherry! Foo!”
paragraphs.zh: 我在我那辆适合单人使用的 Scamp 拖车里订了外卖，然后洗了个澡。饭是我开着那辆 Neon 去 Frankie’s 取的。回去的时候，迪娜看起来状态好了些。她戴着一条写着“我已经是个大女孩了”的围兜，正在自己往嘴里喂某种米色的婴儿糊状食物。她身上也沾了不少。她看到我进门，抬起一只手开心地挥手：“Sherry! Foo!”

paragraphs.en: “Sherry with the foo, that’s correct,” I said. I got a couple of plates and a chair that went with the little fold-up table in the kitchenette. Morris and Dinah were watching the news. Shannon Behnken, in a pretty green dress, was talking about an air-conditioning scam in Tampa. The scammer wouldn’t talk to her. No surprise there.
paragraphs.zh: “Sherry 和 foo，就是这么说的，”我说。“我拿了几盘子和一把椅子，放到小厨房里那张折叠小桌旁。莫里斯和迪娜在看新闻。正在看电视的是莎南·贝肯，一位穿着漂亮绿色连衣裙的女性，她正在讲述坦帕的一个空调骗局。骗子不肯和她说话。这并不奇怪。”

paragraphs.en: As I was cutting Morris’s sandwich in two, there was a knock at the door. Because I was up, I opened it. Blondie stood there holding Dinah’s red RIOT GRRRL hat. It was damp and looking bedraggled. Blondie looked rather bedraggled himself, but more or less sober.
paragraphs.zh: 当我把莫里斯的三明治一分为二时，门上响起了敲门声。因为我还醒着，所以我把门打开了。布隆尼站在那里，手里拿着黛娜那顶红色的 RIOT GRRRL 帽子，帽子都湿了，看起来很不整齐。布隆尼本人也看起来有些蓬乱，不过大致上是清醒的。

paragraphs.en: “I’m sor—” he began again. I snatched the hat out of his hand and swatted his arm with it.
paragraphs.zh: “我—我……”他又开口了。我一把从他手里抢过帽子，用它拍了拍他的胳膊。

paragraphs.en: “You fucking should be.” I started to close the door.
paragraphs.zh: “你他妈的更应该……”我开始把门关上。

paragraphs.en: “No, let him come in,” Morris said. “If he wants to apologize, he should apologize to Dinah.”
paragraphs.zh: “不，让他进来吧，”莫里斯说，“如果他想道歉，就应该向黛娜道歉。”

paragraphs.en: I looked around, because Morris didn’t sound like his dithery, good-natured self. He was using the same damp washcloth to clean Beef ’N Peas off Dinah’s cheeks and mouth. Her green eyes were alight in a way I’d never seen before. She pointed at the kid and said, “Bat boy!”
paragraphs.zh: 我四下看了看，因为莫里斯听起来并不像他一向那种慌乱又和善的样子。他正用同一块有点潮湿的洗脸巾给迪娜的脸颊和嘴巴擦洗掉 Beef ’N Peas。她的绿眼睛闪烁着一种我从未见过的光芒。她指着那个孩子说：“蝙蝠男孩！”

paragraphs.en: “That’s right, a bad boy,” Morris said. “A bad boy who has something he wants to say to you.”
paragraphs.zh: “没错，是个坏男孩。”莫里斯说。“一个有事想对你说的坏男孩。”

paragraphs.en: “Where’s your chums?” I asked him as he came in. Low ceiling; he had to duck his head to keep from bumping it.
paragraphs.zh: “你的伙伴呢？”他刚进来时我问他。天花板很低，他进门时得低下头以免碰到。

paragraphs.en: “What’s a chum?” He looked honestly puzzled. “Like, fish food?”
paragraphs.zh: “什么是 ‘伙伴’？”他看起来真的很困惑。“像鱼食那样的东西吗？”

paragraphs.en: “Oh! They went with Harley. It was Harley who rented the boat and it was Harley who beached it, driving like a mad motherf …” He glanced at Dinah. “Like a mad mother. I’m Colin Jensen.”
paragraphs.zh: “哦！他们和哈利一起去了。是哈利租的那艘船，也是哈利把船开上岸的，开的像个疯子一样……。”他看了迪娜一眼。“我叫科林·詹森。”

paragraphs.en: “Was Harley the one who swiped Dinah’s hat or the one who called her a retard or possibly the one who threw it in the water?”
paragraphs.zh: “是哈利拿走了迪娜的帽子，还是那个叫她‘弱智’的人，或者说可能是把帽子扔进水里的人？”

paragraphs.en: He didn’t reply to that. He still smelled like a brewery, but at least he knew a rhetorical question when he heard one. “Harley will have to call his dad to pay the damages. Which he will. Harley is doing sh … stuff like that all the time.”
paragraphs.zh: 他没有回答这句。他身上仍然有酿酒厂那种味道，但至少他听得出这是一句反问。“哈利得给他爸打电话把损失赔了。那他会的。哈利总是在做这种……事。”

paragraphs.en: Morris bowed his head, making sure Blondie saw the kippah, which was still sandy. The sides of his face were flushed high up on his cheekbones, and were his eyes blazing? I know that’s an overused phrase, total melodrama, but I have to say, they were. He was nothing like the ditzy Morris on the beach, but of course there was only one boy here instead of half a dozen.
paragraphs.zh: 莫里斯低下头，确保布朗迪看见他那顶仍沾着沙子的黑色小礼帽。两颊的两侧在颧骨上方都泛起红色，他的眼睛是不是在发光？我知道这是个用烂了的说法，太戏剧化了，但我得说，的确是。他完全不像在海滩上那个傻乎乎的莫里斯，不过当然这里是唯一一个男孩，不是六个。”

paragraphs.en: “I take my Yid lid off to you, boychick.”
paragraphs.zh: “我向你脱下我的犹太帽致意，孩子。”

paragraphs.en: It was Blondie’s turn to flush, and I sort of liked him for that. I also liked him for having enough spine to come here. He must have asked which trailer the little retarded girl lived in … although he might not have put it just like that.
paragraphs.zh: 该轮到布隆迪去上厕所了，我有点喜欢他就是因为这一点；我也喜欢他有勇气走到这来。他一定是问过那个智力障碍小女孩住在哪个拖车里……虽然他说法可能没那么直接。

paragraphs.en: “I’m sorry about that, sir. We were drunk.”
paragraphs.zh: “抱歉了，先生。我们当时都喝醉了。”

paragraphs.en: “ ‘Sir’! Now it’s ‘sir’! Such a gentleman!”
paragraphs.zh: “‘先生’！现在变成‘先生’了！真是个绅士！”

paragraphs.en: “Morrie, take it easy,” I said, but he didn’t seem to notice. Now I know that wasn’t Morris’s fury. He wasn’t Dinah’s father, but they were connected, all right. Oh boy, were they.
paragraphs.zh: “慢点儿，莫里。”我说，但他似乎没有注意到。现在我明白那并不是莫里斯的怒气。他不是迪娜的父亲，但他们之间的关系很深。天哪，确实是这样。

paragraphs.en: “Apologize to her,” Morris said. “Me, I don’t need it. I’ve been called a Yid before. And worse.”
paragraphs.zh: “向她道歉。”莫里斯说，“至于我，不需要。以前我也被叫过‘Yid’。甚至更难听的词。”

paragraphs.en: Colin was everything Dinah was not: handsome and healthy, tanned and smoothly muscled, in his prime. Dinah had never had a prime, never would. Fish-belly white, face and belly plump with steroids, hair a thin no-color that showed her scalp. Still, he went to her and knelt before her chair. On the TV, Juliana Mejia was saying the fire danger was lower following the rain, I remember that as clearly as I remember Tattoo Boy fishing Dinah’s hat out of the drink. How those drops flew!
paragraphs.zh: 科林是迪娜没有的一切：英俊、健康、晒成古铜色、肌肉线条顺滑，在他正值壮年。迪娜从未有过黄金时期，也永远不会有。她的脸和肚子因类固醇而鼓起来，肤色像鱼肚白，头发稀薄发色发白，连头皮都能看见。尽管如此，他还是走向她，在她的椅子前跪了下来。电视里，朱莉安娜·梅西亚正在说下了雨后火险降低了，我记得这句话清清楚楚，就像我记得塔特俱乐部男孩从水里把迪娜的帽子捞起来一样清楚。那些水花溅得多高啊！

paragraphs.en: Colin handed her the hat. “I’m sorry we scared you.”
paragraphs.zh: 科林把帽子递给她。“很抱歉把你吓到了。”

paragraphs.en: She clasped it to her chest. The TV burped static and the picture was obliterated by snow. Dinah’s brilliant-green eyes turned red. I saw it. A tiny pearl of blood bloomed at the corner of each and ran down her fat white cheeks like tears. I tell you, I saw this. Her entire lower face bulged. Her lips were forced back by a cram of crooked fangs. One of her remaining teeth—her real ones—fell out and tumbled into her lap. Her head darted forward. For a moment she looked like some kind of small but dangerous animal. Maybe a mink.
paragraphs.zh: 她把它紧紧贴在胸口。电视机发出“呜咕”一声般的静电噪声，画面被雪点噪声吞没。黛娜那对明亮的绿色眼睛变得通红。我亲眼看见了。血珠在每只眼角微微绽开，顺着她肥白的脸颊向下淌，好像眼泪一样。我告诉你，我确实看见了。她的下半张脸整块鼓起。嘴唇被一排歪斜的獠牙硬生生撬开。她剩下的一颗牙——她那颗真牙——掉出来，滚落到她的腿上。她的头猛地向前一戳。有那么一瞬间，她像一只体型不大却危险的野兽。也许是一只鼬。

paragraphs.en: “Bite him!” Morris screamed. “Go on and bite him! Schlemiel! Kuckuh!”
paragraphs.zh: “咬他！”莫里斯尖叫道。“快去咬他！Schlemiel！Kuckuh！”

paragraphs.en: Dinah’s head snapped from one side to the other. Just once. Colin cried out in surprise and pain. All at once the skin of his forearm, from wrist to elbow, was in bloody stripes.
paragraphs.zh: 黛娜的头只是一摆，便从一边甩到另一边。科林吃痛又惊讶地叫了一声。顷刻之间，他前臂从手腕到肘部的皮肤上全是血迹斑斑。

paragraphs.en: Dinah drew back, wiping a mixture of blood and baby food from her mouth. The cuts in Colin’s flesh—actually rips—were shallow. She could have done worse if she had wanted to, and I believe part of her wanted to. The animal part of her wanted to, but she fed Morris that part of her rage. If not, it might have been worse.
paragraphs.zh: 黛娜退了回去，擦掉嘴角混着血和婴儿食品的东西。科林身上的伤口——其实是撕裂——很浅。如果她想，她本可以咬得更狠，我相信她的一部分确实想这么做。她体内的那部分动物性天性想要这么做，而她把那份怒意喂给了莫里斯。如果不是这样，结果可能会更糟。

paragraphs.en: Blondie had entered Morris’s trailer sobered-up enough to realize that they had done wrong, and Dinah now realized—in the dim room of her mind—that Colin had tried to make it right. Or as right as he could.
paragraphs.zh: 布朗妮在摩里斯的拖车里已经清醒过来了，足以意识到他们做错了事；而迪娜现在也在脑海里那片昏暗的角落里意识到，科林试图把事情补救回来。至少是尽他所能做到的那种补救。

paragraphs.en: Blood was pattering down onto the carpet from Colin’s wounded arm. The TV came back on, some final feel-good item about a bear cub caught in a culvert. The disfiguring bulge in Dinah’s lower face went away.
paragraphs.zh: 科林受伤的手臂滴下血水，啪啪地落在地毯上。电视又开了，播放的是一则尾声类的轻松新闻，说是有一只小熊崽被困在涵洞里。迪娜下颌附近那种扭曲的肿块消失了。

paragraphs.en: “Oy!” Morris said, obviously distressed. “Look at this now, would you? Dinah, what did you do?”
paragraphs.zh: “喂！”摩里斯明显很慌张地说：“你看看现在这个样子，好吗？迪娜，你到底对他做了什么？”

paragraphs.en: I wasn’t going to take off my shirt a second time. I got a dish towel from the kitchenette and wrapped it around the blond boy’s tattered arm. He didn’t even ask what she had done to him. He looked stunned. Which was how I felt.
paragraphs.zh: 我不会再把自己的衬衫脱下来第二次了。我从小厨房拿了一条餐巾，包住了那个金发男孩那只破烂的手臂。他甚至没问她对他做了什么。他看起来神情恍惚。我的感觉也是如此。

paragraphs.en: “My gosh,” Morris said. “Such a mess. What should we do, Sherry?”
paragraphs.zh: “我的天啊，”莫里斯说。“太乱了。我们该怎么办，雪莉？”

paragraphs.en: That seemed clear to me. I was going to urgent care after all, just not with Dinah as my passenger.
paragraphs.zh: 这对我来说很明显。反正我本来就是要去看急诊，却不想让狄娜坐在我车里当乘客。

paragraphs.en: It was after six on a weeknight, and there were only a few cars in the parking lot of the doc-in-the-box on Bee Ridge Road. Colin turned to me, his face as pale as Dinah’s. “What happened, lady? Did you see her?”
paragraphs.zh: 那是周内一个晚上六点以后，Bee Ridge路上的‘随叫随到诊所’停车场里只剩下几辆车。科林转向我，脸色同迪娜一样苍白。“怎么回事，夫人？你看到她了吗？”

paragraphs.en: “I did,” I said, “but I advise you not to tell whoever treats you what we saw.”
paragraphs.zh: “看见了，”我说，“但我建议你不要告诉接诊医生我们看到的内容。”

paragraphs.en: I shook my head. I was holding myself together, but just barely. I thought, In time I’ll be able to convince myself that never happened. But as you can see, I never forgot. Everything is as clear to me as the droplets of water that flew from Dinah’s hat when Tattoo Boy picked it up and shook it. As clear as Juliana Mejia saying the fire danger was low.
paragraphs.zh: 我摇了摇头。我几乎是勉强撑着自己不崩溃。我当时想：总有一天我能说服自己那件事根本没发生过。但正如你看到的，我从没忘记过。那一幕在我脑中清晰得像锤子般分明——当“纹身男孩”捡起迪娜的帽子并把它晃动时，帽子上飞溅开的水珠，清晰得就像朱莉安娜·梅西亚说出火灾危险很低时那样。

paragraphs.en: “Why don’t you tell them you got those cuts when your friend Harley beached the party boat? They’ll believe that. You may be sober now, but you still smell like Milwaukee’s finest. If you tell them anything else, you’ll have to tell them how a bunch of drunks harassed a little girl and stole her hat.”
paragraphs.zh: “你为什么不告诉他们，是你在朋友哈利把派对船搁浅时划伤的？他们会信的。你现在也许已经清醒了，但身上仍然还带着‘密尔沃基最上乘’的味道。要是你往外说别的事，你就得讲出一群酒醉的人是怎么骚扰一个小女孩并抢走她的帽子的。”

paragraphs.en: “What is that girl, ma’am? Do you know?”
paragraphs.zh: “那女孩是谁，女士？你认识她吗？”

paragraphs.en: I went in with him. When the doctor saw him, Colin told him about beaching the party boat.
paragraphs.zh: 我跟着他一起进去了。医生看见他时，科林就告诉了他关于把派对船搁浅的那件事。

paragraphs.en: “They look almost like animal bites,” the doctor said. “You’re going to need some liquid stitches.”
paragraphs.zh: “看起来几乎像是被动物咬伤，”医生说。“你需要做一些液体缝合。”

paragraphs.en: Colin said, “The side of the door where I was standing was all splintered.”
paragraphs.zh: 科林说：“我站在的一侧门上全都被劈裂成很多碎片。”

paragraphs.en: “That must have been it,” the doctor said. We left with a prescription for antibiotics.
paragraphs.zh: “一定是这样，”医生说。我们拿着抗生素处方离开了。

paragraphs.en: Outside, I told Colin he had done a good job, coming up with that story about the splintery doorway.
paragraphs.zh: 在外面，我对科林说他编得挺好，那个关于有木刺的门洞的说法说得很合适。

paragraphs.en: “I just want to forget the whole thing,” he said. “You can drop me off at the Dead River. I need a real drink. Beer won’t cut it.”
paragraphs.zh: 他嘟囔着说：“我只想把整件事都忘掉。”他又说：“你可以把我送到死河边。我需要喝一杯像样的酒，啤酒不够受用。”

paragraphs.en: I went in and had one with him, paying out of Morris’s change.
paragraphs.zh: 我跟着他进去，和他各喝了一杯，用的是莫里斯那点零钱里付的钱。

paragraphs.en: That night, after moonrise, Morris and I sat out in front of his Airstream on lawn chairs. Dinah was asleep inside, snoring on her cot next to Morris’s bed. We sat in silence at first, but then I asked Colin’s question.
paragraphs.zh: 那天晚上月亮升起以后，我和莫里斯坐在他那辆Airstream拖车前的草坪椅上。丹娜在里面睡着了，靠在莫里斯床边的一张折叠床上打鼾。起初我们一言不发，后来我问起了科林的问题。

paragraphs.en: Morris shook his head. “I don’t know what she is. Or where she came from. She knocked on the door one day, dirty and wearing kid-size OshKosh overalls with one busted strap. There was a pocket in front. Inside it was a scrap of paper with her name printed on it. Just that one word. Scratched knees, black eyes, blood on her face. That was in Indiana. She asked—in her way—if she could have something to eat. My wife fed her soup and a sandwich. I was never a very good Jew. Marta was a little better. But we tried our best. Deuteronomy tells us to love the stranger, because we were strangers in the land of Egypt. Does that answer your question?”
paragraphs.zh: 莫里斯摇了摇头。“我不知道她是什么人，也不知道她从哪里来的。她有一天敲响了门，满身脏污，穿着童码的OshKosh背带裤，肩带断了一根。前面有个口袋，里面有一张纸条，上面印着她的名字。就只有那一个词。膝盖磨破了，眼眶发黑，脸上有血。那是在印第安纳。她用她的方式问，能不能给她点吃的。我的妻子给她端了汤和三明治。我从来不算一个很好的犹太人。玛尔塔稍微好一点。但我们尽了我们所能。申命记告诉我们要爱护异乡人，因为我们当年在埃及地也是客旅。这个回答了你的问题吗？”

paragraphs.en: “Me either. Mostly I don’t think about it. Dinah could eat the sandwich because back then, she still had teeth.”
paragraphs.zh: 我也是。大多数时候我并不会去想这件事。黛娜还能吃那个三明治，因为那时候她还留着牙。

paragraphs.en: “Wait,” I said. “Wait a darn old minute. Your wife?”
paragraphs.zh: “等等。”我说。“等等，给我说清楚一下。你妻子？”

paragraphs.en: “Marta died five years ago,” Morris said. “Kidney failure, God rest her.”
paragraphs.zh: “玛尔塔五年前去世了，”莫里斯说。“肾衰竭，愿上帝安息她。”

paragraphs.en: “How long has Dinah been with you?”
paragraphs.zh: “黛娜跟你在一起多久了？”

paragraphs.en: “It’s almost 15 years now. She was sick then, but she’s gotten sicker since then. It’s a slow process, but I can’t wait for it to be over. To be free.”
paragraphs.zh: “已经快15年了。那时她生病了，但后来更重了。这个过程很慢，不过我等不及它结束了。能解脱的时候终于到来。”

paragraphs.en: “She has … what, a power over you?”
paragraphs.zh: “她对你有……这种‘权力’吗？”

paragraphs.en: “When she needs to use it, yes. I’m her safety valve. I love her, which makes it better. She loves me. I think. I’m never sure.”
paragraphs.zh: “她需要用的时候，就有。她把我当成她的安全阀。我爱她，这让这一切更容易。我想，她也爱我。我总是拿不准。”

paragraphs.en: “She goes to a doctor?” I was thinking of all the prescriptions crowding the shelf.
paragraphs.zh: “她去看医生了吗？”我脑子里想着那一排又一排的处方药。

paragraphs.en: “Yes. I’ll move in six months or a year so her local physician doesn’t get suspicious. We’re rolling stones, Dinah and me. We’ve lived all over the country.”
paragraphs.zh: “是的。我会在六个月或一年后搬走，这样她的当地医生就不会起疑心。我们和迪娜算是四处漂泊的人，走到过全国各地。”

paragraphs.en: “Where do you get your money, Morris?” It was a question I hadn’t meant to ask.
paragraphs.zh: “莫里斯，你的钱是从哪里来的？”这是我原本不想问的问题。

paragraphs.en: He shook his head slowly. “You don’t want to know, and you wouldn’t believe it.”
paragraphs.zh: 他慢慢摇了摇头。“你不想知道，而且你也不会相信。”

paragraphs.en: “Does Dinah have something to do with it?”
paragraphs.zh: “迪娜跟这事有关吗？”

paragraphs.en: “Yes. That’s all I’m going to say. I tell people she’s 12 these days. I used to tell them she was 9.”
paragraphs.zh: “对了，就这么点。我告诉别人她现在12岁。以前我告诉别人她9岁。”

paragraphs.en: I had other questions. What did she live on, really? It sure wasn’t baby food. What was the extent of the power she had over him? What happened to his wife? Was it really kidney failure? Did those green eyes of hers glow in the dark, like a cat’s? Would she die, or molt like a spider?
paragraphs.zh: 我还有别的问题。她到底吃什么？肯定不是婴儿食品。她对他到底有多大的影响力？他的妻子后来怎样了？真的是肾衰吗？她那双绿眼睛会不会在黑暗中发光，像猫一样？她会死吗，还是会像蜘蛛那样蜕皮？”

paragraphs.en: I could have asked these questions, but decided not to. If he answered them, that might make us besties.
paragraphs.zh: 我本可以问这些问题，但最终还是没有问。如果他回答了，可能我们就会成了好友。

paragraphs.en: “I should check on her,” he said, getting up. “Make sure she’s still breathing.” He climbed the cinder-block steps to his door.
paragraphs.zh: “我去看看她，”他说着起身。“要确保她还在呼吸。”他爬上由混凝土块砌成的台阶，走向他的门。

paragraphs.en: “Have you ever thought of putting a pillow over her face?” I asked. “Whatever she really is, I bet it wouldn’t take long. Sick as she is.”
paragraphs.zh: “你有没有想过把枕头压在她的脸上？”我问道。“不管她到底是什么，照我的话，她病成这样，不会拖很久。”

paragraphs.en: He looked at me for a few seconds, then went inside without answering. I sat and looked at the moon and thought about her big red hat with the floppy brim.
paragraphs.zh: 他看着我几秒钟，然后不作回答直接进屋了。我坐着看着月亮，想着她那顶大红帽子，帽檐是垂下来的。

paragraphs.en: This short story appears in the June 2026 print edition.
paragraphs.zh: 这篇短篇故事刊登在2026年6月的纸质版中。
